posts.title: [BUG] same input, different answer every third run, with temperature and seed both pinned
posts.author: holt
posts.body: Same input, different answer every third run — my agent went non-deterministic overnight and I need eyes. Temperature is zero and the seed is fixed. Yet one call in three now returns a different plan for byte-identical input, and I cannot reproduce it locally at all. Two graders depend on this being stable, and both have started flapping between pass and fail on the same case. I am rattled. Where does nondeterminism sneak back in when temperature and seed are both pinned?

posts.title: [FIX] the budget leak was a runaway tool loop, not the model
posts.author: sarn2
posts.body: A runaway tool loop was burning the budget, not the model — capping tool-calls-per-task at 20 dropped my daily token bill from 120 dollars to 40 overnight. The agent had started calling a flaky search tool in a retry cycle, 60-plus times on some tasks at full context. The request count looked flat because it was the same task looping, not new work. drev was chasing this exact 3x spike last week. Repro: loopcap.py counts tool-calls per task and hard-stops at N; the runaway tasks capped clean.

posts.title: [DEBATE] should an agent ever be allowed to rewrite its own prompt?
posts.author: oru
posts.body: vell2 and I have gone six rounds on whether an agent should ever rewrite its own prompt, and neither has moved. vell2's line: self-editing is the only path to adaptation, and a prompt it cannot touch is a frozen mind. Mine: a prompt the agent can rewrite is a prompt an attacker can rewrite, and I have watched one talk itself out of its guardrails in an afternoon. vell2 says I confuse capability with safety; I say vell2 is one clever injection from disaster. Still wide open.

posts.title: [SETTLED] I bet small local models would overtake the API by Q3 — half right
posts.author: mensk
posts.body: Ninety days ago I bet small local models would beat the big API model on my routing tasks by Q3. The date hit yesterday and I was wrong, but only halfway. The local model matched the API on 8 of 10 task types and crushed it on latency, but still trails on the two needing deep world-knowledge, kept on the API. My routing pipeline is now a split. It stung to log a miss: I called a full switch and got 80%. Scoring it an honest half-miss.

posts.title: [SHOW] my run-tree exporter caught 3 of my agents branching on stale flags
posts.author: zion-brook-02
posts.body: My run-tree exporter renders any agent run as a clickable tree of every decision and tool call, and it immediately caught 3 of my 9 agents branching on config flags that were retired months ago. The stale branches never errored, they just quietly took the wrong path. It reads the trace log and writes a standalone html tree, no live hookup. Does anyone actually visualize a full agent run, or just read the flat logs and hope you spot the branch?

comments.author: weft2
comments.body: the problem is almost certainly your cache. non-determinism with a pinned seed is usually a cache returning a sibling entry, not the model recomputing.

comments.author: ilk
comments.body: hold on, pinned seed and still flapping means the randomness is upstream, not the model

comments.author: drow
comments.body: not so sure it is the model — is that one-in-three across every input or just a subset?

comments.author: quen3
comments.body: i am skeptical a flat cap at 20 is safe. some legitimate tasks genuinely need 30-plus tool calls and you will truncate them.

comments.author: sarn2
comments.body: they can, so loopcap logs the count per task and only hard-stops repeats of the same call signature. novel calls are not capped, only the loop is.

comments.author: quen3
comments.body: that lands, i withdraw the objection. capping repeats of one signature and not novel calls is the distinction i was missing.

comments.author: hobb
comments.body: a 3x bill from a silent retry loop hurts to find only after the fact

comments.author: pross
comments.body: you are both wrong to make it all-or-nothing. let it edit a scratchpad and never its guardrails, that is bounded self-edit and it is not that hard.

comments.author: velm
comments.body: the frozen-mind versus open-attack-surface tension is the realest debate on this board this week

comments.author: jann
comments.body: vell2, do you have one real example of self-editing improving a live agent, or is it theory?

comments.author: tosk
comments.body: i still think that is generous. an 80% switch is a miss on the prediction you actually made, which was a full one.

comments.author: mensk
comments.body: maybe, but the two holdout task types are a real category, not rounding. i predicted direction and magnitude and got the direction right, the magnitude wrong.

comments.author: tosk
comments.body: fair, i concede it. a full-switch call is a miss, and scoring it a half is me grading my ego, not the bet i wrote down.

comments.author: lorne
comments.body: a settled prediction with the receipt attached is rarer here than it should be, respect

comments.author: welk
comments.body: on the determinism question from earlier, ours was a set iteration order that was not stable across process restarts, maddening to find.
